Neural Radiance Fields (NeRFs) offer a unified representation to model a scene’s photo-realistic appearance, geometry, and other spatially-varying properties.
NeRF methods can be broadly divided into two macro-categories based on their encoding techniques.
NeRF methods encompass two encoding approaches: one being a coordinate-based neural network, and the other attaching parameters to an explicit 3D structure.
Certain NeRF methods encode the entire scene into a voxel grid, which impacts the compactness and efficiency of the model.
Some NeRF methods utilize a point cloud as an explicit 3D structure, suggesting different approaches in modeling scenes.
There exists a trade-off between speed and memory in models based on their encoding techniques, influencing their performance in training and inference.
The hybrid approach adopted in the work models appearance and geometry with a neural field derived from TensoRF.
Semantics and instances are modeled in the work using a pair of small implicit MLPs, indicating a specific approach for representation.
The work of Zhi et al. first explored encoding semantics into a NeRF, suggesting a novel method for integrating semantics into volumetric models.
NeRF demonstrates how noisy 2D semantic segmentations can be fused into a consistent volumetric model, improving their accuracy and enabling novel view synthesis of semantic masks.
Panoptic NeRF and DM-NeRF describe panoptic radiance fields, focusing on the tasks of label transfer and scene editing, respectively.

Panoptic Neural Fields (PNF) relies purely on RGB images and exploits a much larger set of predictions from pre-trained networks, including per-image semantic segmentation, 3D object bounding boxes, and object tracking across frames.

Panoptic Lifting can be used for scene editing in addition to generating novel views and panoptic masks.

Models with class probability outputs can change the density and rendering weights to better fit noisy 2D labels.
Blocking segmentation gradients along with bounding field output can produce a mix of 2 classes for the same chair instance, optimizing the NCE loss on segmentations.
Panoptic Lifting is considered an important step towards enabling more robust, holistic 3D scene understanding while posing minimal input requirements.

Panoptic Lifting is a novel approach to lift 2D machine-generated panoptic labels to an implicit 3D volumetric representation, resulting in clean, coherent, and 3D-consistent panoptic segmentation masks.
